acceptance_criteria: Support Cockpit is documented as the selected prototype direction in pack-local state and specs.
The Starlight publishing path begins moving toward the Support Cockpit interaction model without changing the JSON-first artifact contract.
The next implementation cycle preserves the sticky quick-jump behavior, collapsible checkpoints, and command-first presentation.
completion_signals: Pack-local objective, backlog, work-state, and readiness files all reflect Support Cockpit as the chosen direction.
The remote Codex runtime is restaged with the updated build-pack state and given a concrete Support Cockpit implementation task.
Remote Codex invocation challenges and mitigations are documented in a pack-local note so the next implementation cycle starts from a cleaner launcher pattern.
escalation_conditions: The Support Cockpit direction starts to require account state, saved case state, or broader product platform work.
The next publishing iteration would replace the machine-readable baseline model instead of rendering it.
tasks.summary: Adopt Support Cockpit as the selected Starlight publishing direction and drive the next implementation cycle through that chassis.

acceptance_criteria: The build-pack defines a canonical artifact model for runtime evidence, service inventory, and baseline output.
The first artifact model clearly separates observed facts, inference, and unknowns.
completion_signals: The CLI and source layout expose named surfaces for the first ADF artifact set.
The pack-local objective, backlog, and work-state files all point at the same first implementation slice.
escalation_conditions: The artifact model cannot stay small enough for the MVP without reopening product scope.
The required output set starts to imply broad test or fixture expansion.
tasks.summary: Define the first bounded ADF artifact model for runtime evidence, service inventory, and baseline output.

acceptance_criteria: The build-pack can collect a safe first-pass AlgoSec runtime snapshot from the lab appliance.
The first collection path stays read-only and bounded to support-relevant evidence classes.
completion_signals: A runtime collection command exists for the first ADF slice.
The collection output includes service inventory and core platform checkpoints.
escalation_conditions: The target appliance requires risky write actions or unsupported privilege changes to gather the first evidence set.
The first-pass evidence set expands beyond the MVP boundary of useful support diagnostics.
tasks.summary: Implement the first safe runtime evidence collection path for the controlled AlgoSec lab appliance.

acceptance_criteria: The build-pack renders machine-readable baseline artifacts into a simple support-facing HTML view.
The HTML output remains a render of canonical JSON artifacts rather than a separate source of truth.
completion_signals: The first HTML-ready baseline output exists for the AlgoSec lab path.
Support-facing output is generated from the same artifact model used for machine-readable evidence.
escalation_conditions: The HTML layer starts to dominate the scope instead of remaining a thin render of machine-readable output.
The support-facing output requires broad UX work beyond a simple MVP render.
tasks.summary: Generate the first HTML-ready support output from canonical ADF baseline artifacts.

acceptance_criteria: The build-pack can generate Astro Starlight site source from the canonical ADF baseline without replacing JSON as the source of truth.
The Rocky lab appliance can install and build the generated Astro Starlight site successfully with the system Node runtime.
The publishing direction stays portable so the long-term rendered site can later move to a separate Ubuntu host.
completion_signals: The build-pack exposes a generate-starlight-site command that writes package.json, Astro config, content collection files, markdown playbook pages, and renderer assets from support-baseline.json.
The lab appliance has a successful Astro Starlight build under the generated starlight-site artifact root.
escalation_conditions: The publishing layer starts to compete with the runtime evidence model instead of rendering it.
The Starlight path requires deep platform or hosting work before the support content is proven.
tasks.summary: Prototype Astro Starlight publishing from the canonical ADF baseline and verify the site can build on the Rocky lab appliance.

acceptance_criteria: The top support domains use language and sequencing that match how Tier 2 support engineers actually diagnose customer issues from an SSH session.
The highest-value decision playbooks are tuned for operator review without changing the canonical JSON-first model.
Priority playbooks use the current Check, Run, Verification, Linux note, and Known working example pattern.
The wording stays plain, short, and easy to follow for engineers using English as a second language.
completion_signals: ASMS UI is down reflects the reviewed SSH-first four-step Linux-first flow inside the cleaned Support Cockpit chassis, and FireFlow Backend follows next.
HTML and JSON outputs remain aligned after the language tuning pass.
escalation_conditions: Requested wording changes imply a broader UX rewrite instead of playbook tuning.
Support guidance starts to require risky write actions instead of read-only diagnostics.
tasks.summary: Continue tuning high-value support playbooks into final SSH-first operator language and decision flow, using the Support Cockpit chassis as the next operator-facing container.

acceptance_criteria: Every major playbook can start from a shared host health pre-check pack before moving into subsystem-specific checks.
Priority playbooks embed service-status, listener, config, and log commands for more AlgoSec services and dependencies.
Each command remains read-only and includes a healthy signal plus failure interpretation.
Priority commands include short Linux note blocks, copy controls, and known working terminal examples where the target appliance can provide them.
Rare path or config checks stay deferred unless a playbook truly needs them.
completion_signals: A shared host health pre-check pack exists for disk, memory, OOM, and related host-resource checks used across major playbooks.
The command bundles extend beyond the current top-path checks and cover more service-specific failure paths.
Service ports, config checkpoints, and known working command output are surfaced more explicitly in the operator-facing output.
The ASMS UI path proves a reusable service-pack pattern with lab-backed status, listener, functional, and file-log checks for Keycloak and ms-metro.
escalation_conditions: The required command coverage implies broad appliance-specific scripting beyond the MVP boundary.
The next command set would require unsupported or risky write access on the target appliance.
tasks.summary: Expand service-specific diagnostic command coverage for priority services and dependencies, starting from the reusable ASMS UI service-pack pattern.

acceptance_criteria: A fixed shared host health pre-check pack is now defined for every major playbook before subsystem-specific checks.
completion_signals: A fixed shared host health pre-check pack is now defined for every major playbook before subsystem-specific checks.

Disk, memory, OOM, and other host-resource checks are foundational Linux triage steps in every scenario. Defining them as a standard reusable pre-check keeps playbooks consistent and helps engineers start in the same place every time.

tasks.summary: A fixed shared host health pre-check pack is now defined for every major playbook before subsystem-specific checks.

completion_signals: Use one real authenticated GUI flow plus same-minute log correlation to prove that the ASMS UI playbook can be refined from observed runtime behavior instead of guessed service order.

This is the first strong proof that ADF's systems-thinking method reduces support ambiguity in a real GUI-down path. The current ASMS UI page proved several valuable patterns, but it still reflects too much service thinking. The next real ADF win is a from-scratch playbook that helps an engineer decide whether the host, Apache/HTTPD serving the UI, auth branch, app branch, and overall path can do useful work.

acceptance_criteria: ADF tracks one explicit launcher-stability task for remote Codex work on the lab appliance instead of leaving the issue scattered across notes and ad hoc terminal recovery.

The next content slice follows the backend map-and-render contract so internal mapping rows, operator-facing rows, page type, and handoff targets are represented explicitly before Starlight rendering.
completion_signals: Shared dependencies and deeper failure branches are clearer for the top customer-facing paths.
The next remote review can focus on specific unresolved service paths instead of the whole appliance.

The next Keycloak refinement, if it lands, produces a reusable imported-module drilldown shape that helps the engineer classify why the module cannot do useful work and what evidence to hand off, rather than trying to encode every Keycloak-specific fix.

The next content pass can point to one explicit page-type contract, one support-language translation contract, one symptom-to-boundary mapping workflow, one support-safe scientific-method contract, and one backend map-and-render contract instead of rediscovering those rules in review.
escalation_conditions: The dependency model starts to force full-system reverse engineering instead of bounded support diagnostics.
The next extension would require broad new tests or fixture matrices.
tasks.summary: Deepen dependency-aware playbooks for the highest-value AlgoSec service paths while keeping the frontline model symptom-first, support-language-first, and explicitly split between symptom-entry playbooks, boundary-confirmation pages, and deep guides.

acceptance_criteria: The next `adf-dev` slice returns one fresh exact later-content minute after `/afa/php/home.php` with at least one stronger later-content marker such as `GET_REPORTS`, `GET_POLICY_TAB`, `GET_DEVICE_POLICY`, `GET_MONITORING_CHANGES`, or `GET_ANALYSIS_OPTIONS`.
The returned checkpoint bundle and exported runtime-evidence bundle are both recoverable locally through the reviewed PackFactory pull/import path.
If a fresh exact later-content minute cannot be preserved, the remote run stops as `blocked_boundary` instead of counting local wording refinement, validation, or benchmark passes as completion.
completion_signals: A fresh exact later-content minute is preserved with `/afa/php/home.php` already present and a stronger later-content marker in the same bounded window.
The checkpoint bundle notes the support stop rule that the case is no longer top-level `GUI down` once that later-content minute is observed.
Restart state tells the next agent that preserved timestamps or local Step 4 wording alone do not satisfy this checkpoint.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the remote slice can only reuse preserved timestamps or local source edits without returning one fresh later-content minute.
Stop as `blocked_boundary` if the reviewed remote workflow cannot export a recoverable runtime-evidence bundle for the later-content checkpoint.
tasks.summary: Capture one fresh exact later-content minute after `/afa/php/home.php` through `adf-dev`, or stop as `blocked_boundary`, so support can branch out of top-level `GUI down` cleanly.

acceptance_criteria: ADF preserves the exact late-export v4 shape where the first wrapper pullback failed but a later reviewed pullback recovered a valid exported bundle and a support-useful checkpoint.
The reviewed PackFactory wrapper or pullback path gains one explicit late-export recovery rule or classifier so this shape no longer looks like a hard failure by default.
Restart state tells the next agent that the later-content checkpoint succeeded, but the autonomy workflow still needs a stronger late-bundle pullback rule.
completion_signals: One bounded note or proof path exists that shows how late export bundle arrival is classified or recovered without losing the checkpoint.
A future wrapper run can stop with a structured late-export result instead of a raw crash when the first delayed pullback is too early.
The next agent no longer has to rediscover that v4 succeeded after the initial wrapper failure.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if improving the late-bundle path requires unsupported ad hoc remote-session supervision instead of PackFactory-local workflow changes.
Stop and escalate if the recovery design starts to widen into a broad remote-runtime redesign before the bounded late-export shape is captured clearly.
tasks.summary: Tune late-export pullback recovery so a slice that exports after the first delayed pullback does not get misclassified as a final wrapper failure.
validation_commands: PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 python3 -m py_compile tools/pull_remote_runtime_evidence.py tools/run_remote_autonomy_test.py tools/run_remote_active_task_continuity_test.py tools/import_external_runtime_evidence.py

acceptance_criteria: ADF preserves the strict later-content startup stall as explicit autonomy evidence instead of conflating it with a content checkpoint failure.
At least one bounded control-plane improvement or fail-closed classifier is identified for runs that stop at `run_started` and never write `run-summary.json`.
Restart state tells the next agent not to retry `capture_fresh_later_content_branch_checkpoint` until the startup stall is either fixed or formally classified.
completion_signals: The v2 and v3 strict later-content slices are recorded as reproducible startup stalls rather than content results.
A next-step control-plane fix or tighter stop rule is named for remote runs that never progress past `run_started`.
The blocked child content task can resume only after that control-plane follow-up is accepted.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if diagnosing the stall would require ad hoc remote-session control outside the reviewed PackFactory workflow family.
Stop and escalate if the next plausible fix expands into broad factory-wide remote-runtime redesign before this bounded ADF slice is explained.
tasks.summary: Diagnose why strict `adf-dev` evidence slices stall after `run_started` and before the first checkpoint so the blocked later-content proof can resume cleanly.

acceptance_criteria: The next `adf-dev` diagnosis slice compares one preserved successful remote checkpoint run shape against the observed strict-slice startup stall and records one concrete control-plane hypothesis or bounded blocker.
The slice stays inside `.pack-state`, `status`, `tasks`, `readiness`, and `eval/history` surfaces and does not retry the blocked later-content content proof directly.
The returned checkpoint stops after preserving one explicit comparison note and one named next fix or classifier, rather than continuing into open-ended launcher research.
completion_signals: A single explicit comparison note exists that names what a successful remote checkpoint run wrote, what the strict stalled runs failed to write, and one bounded next fix or classifier.
Restart state tells the next agent to prefer this comparison-first diagnosis slice over another broad startup-stall investigation prompt.
The blocked later-content task stays blocked until the comparison result is accepted.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if even this narrowed comparison slice cannot produce a checkpoint without drifting into open-ended remote-session analysis.
Stop and escalate if the next plausible fix would require unsupported ad hoc remote-session control instead of PackFactory-local workflow changes.
tasks.summary: Compare one successful remote checkpoint run shape against the strict startup-stall runs and preserve one bounded control-plane hypothesis before retrying any content slice.

acceptance_criteria: ADF has one explicitly named evidence-only next remote slice for `deepen_dependency_aware_playbooks` that fits the current raw bounded loop writable-surface contract.
The planned slice stays on the first usable shell versus Metro-backed home-shell boundary and limits remote mutations to state, history, memory, or candidate/runtime-evidence artifacts.

The returned checkpoint bundle names one exact evidence minute by timestamp or narrow window, `SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php -> /afa/php/home.php` shell transition, at least two nearby Metro clues from that same minute, and a support stop-point judgment instead of stopping at continuity bookkeeping alone.
completion_signals: The next remote slice is defined as a first-usable-shell versus Metro evidence pass that can run without another `unauthorized_writable_surface` failure.
Restart state tells the next agent not to rerun a raw source-authoring slice under the unchanged contract.

The exported checkpoint bundle and operator notes identify at least one exact shell-transition evidence minute, at least two same-minute Metro clues, and a support stop-point judgment rather than only a measurement or pause boundary.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the next useful remote slice still requires direct `src/` or `docs/` authoring before the contract is reviewed.
Stop as `blocked_boundary` if the slice cannot return one exact shell-transition evidence minute with same-minute Metro clues and a support stop rule.
tasks.summary: Define and prove the next evidence-only `adf-dev` checkpoint slice on the first usable shell versus Metro-backed home-shell boundary so it stays inside the current raw bounded loop writable-surface contract and returns one exact evidence-bearing checkpoint minute.

acceptance_criteria: The remote autonomy loop no longer reports leftover `starter_backlog_incomplete` terminal wording when it is evaluating a real current-task continuity run for ADF.
Terminal outcome and reason language align with the current-task contract so later `adf-dev` reruns are easier to score and optimize.
completion_signals: A later `adf-dev` rerun shows current-task-based terminal semantics instead of the older starter-backlog wording.
ADF can distinguish the already-fixed prompt and writable-surface blockers from the later terminal-semantics refinement in its planner and restart state.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if aligning terminal semantics would require widening the remote control plane beyond bounded PackFactory workflows.
tasks.summary: Align the residual `adf-dev` remote-loop terminal-state wording with the current-task continuity contract after the main contract blockers are already fixed.

acceptance_criteria: The dependency path now renders horizontally within the center pane so it uses width instead of pushing the stepwise details farther down the page.
completion_signals: The dependency path now renders horizontally within the center pane so it uses width instead of pushing the stepwise details farther down the page.
This recovers vertical room for the detailed checks while preserving the path-map view at the top of the playbook.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if this task would require widening ADF scope, unsupported PackFactory mutations, or unreviewed risk.
tasks.summary: The dependency path now renders horizontally within the center pane so it uses width instead of pushing the stepwise details farther down the page.

acceptance_criteria: Expand the collapsible Linux note pattern with more short educational context so Tier 2 support engineers can learn unfamiliar Linux terms without blocking the main troubleshooting flow.
completion_signals: Expand the collapsible Linux note pattern with more short educational context so Tier 2 support engineers can learn unfamiliar Linux terms without blocking the main troubleshooting flow.

The current Linux notes are useful because they are optional. Engineers can skip them when they already know the concept, or open them when they need a quick explanation. This can build shared Linux troubleshooting language over time without turning the playbook into training material.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if this task would require widening ADF scope, unsupported PackFactory mutations, or unreviewed risk.
tasks.summary: Expand the collapsible Linux note pattern with more short educational context so Tier 2 support engineers can learn unfamiliar Linux terms without blocking the main troubleshooting flow.

acceptance_criteria: Record a canonical ASMS-runtime-first taxonomy that future ADF playbooks share, with Linux utilities and host state treated as supporting evidence surfaces.
Make ADF intentionally push engineers toward system thinking, where the goal is to determine whether a component, subsystem, or the whole system can do useful work, not only whether a process is up.
completion_signals: The build-pack records a canonical ASMS-runtime-first taxonomy and uses it as the shared frame for future playbooks.
ADF treats `useful work` as a stop rule rather than as a named layer in that taxonomy.
This is the deeper value behind the recent service-pack work. ADF is strongest when it helps engineers judge productive system behavior rather than memorize isolated service checks.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if this task would require widening ADF scope, unsupported PackFactory mutations, or unreviewed risk.
tasks.summary: Capture a canonical ASMS-runtime-first taxonomy so ADF pushes engineers toward system thinking, where the goal is to determine whether a component, subsystem, or the whole system can do useful work, not only whether a process is up.

acceptance_criteria: Decide how explicitly ADF should treat host integration and Linux operating evidence as supporting diagnostic surfaces for ASMS runtime troubleshooting, rather than as the first architectural layer.
completion_signals: Host integration and Linux operating evidence are treated as explicit supporting surfaces in the shared taxonomy, not only as recurring pre-check habits.

ADF is already trending toward Linux-first diagnostics, Linux-standard language, and Linux note clarifications. Formalizing that foundation would help align playbooks, terminology, discovery logic, and future agent guidance around the real operating context engineers face during triage.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if this task would require widening ADF scope, unsupported PackFactory mutations, or unreviewed risk.
tasks.summary: Formalize host integration and Linux operating evidence as supporting surfaces in ADF's ASMS-runtime-first taxonomy, not as the first architectural layer.

acceptance_criteria: ADF records one explicit task plus adjacent specification for revising frontline publication contracts toward a flow-first operator model that fits the actual AlgoSec support workflow.
The new spec says plainly that the operator-facing page should optimize for diagnostic narrowing and branch clarity rather than mandatory wrapper sections like `Use this when` or `What to save`.
The spec keeps backend structure and systems-thinking logic intact while moving backend-facing metadata and extra workflow scaffolding behind the scenes by default.
completion_signals: ADF has one named contract task for realigning the publication model to the actual support workflow instead of treating the mismatch as a renderer-only tweak.
The adjacent spec names which existing contracts should be tightened or reinterpreted and which ones should remain intact.
Later renderer and contract implementation can judge success against one explicit flow-first publication contract.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the new contract starts to weaken branch clarity, stop-point logic, or backend evidence preservation instead of only trimming unnecessary operator scaffolding.
Stop and escalate if the contract widens into a broad product rewrite rather than a bounded realignment of publication assumptions to the existing support workflow.
tasks.summary: Realign ADF frontline publication contracts to a flow-first operator model that keeps diagnostic narrowing and branch clarity visible while moving backend-facing metadata and extra workflow scaffolding behind the scenes.

acceptance_criteria: ADF records one explicit verification task for confirming that `ASMS Keycloak auth is down` really adheres to the canonical boundary-playbook template, not just that it is newer than the remaining support-cockpit pages.

The review checks the live rendered page against the canonical shell expectations for page-record ownership, support-language-first flow, command flow shape, handoff behavior, and allowed supplement layering.
If the page still contains drift from the canonical shell, the verification task records the mismatch clearly enough that follow-up migration work does not copy a flawed reference page.
completion_signals: ADF has one named planner checkpoint that proves whether `ASMS Keycloak auth is down` is the correct canonical reference page for future boundary-playbook migration.
The next migration pass can treat the Keycloak page as a trusted template target only after this verification is complete.
Support review can distinguish between `newer page` and `canonical page` instead of assuming they are automatically the same.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the review reveals that the Keycloak page still depends on one-off renderer behavior that the canonical shell is not supposed to require.
Stop and escalate if the verification would turn into open-ended page redesign instead of a bounded contract check against the named canonical template.
tasks.summary: Confirm that `ASMS Keycloak auth is down` adheres to the canonical boundary-playbook template before treating it as the migration reference for the remaining subsystem playbooks.

acceptance_criteria: The `messaging-and-data`, `microservice-platform`, and `core-aff` published playbooks stop rendering through the older support-cockpit-only template path and publish through the same canonical boundary-playbook shell now used by `keycloak-auth`.
The migrated pages still preserve their boundary-specific supplements, likely-service context, and command flow without losing the newer generated page-record contract.

The rendered site no longer presents mixed first-class playbook templates where `ASMS Keycloak auth is down` uses the new canonical shell but the other named subsystem playbooks still use the older support cockpit shell.
completion_signals: The remaining three subsystem playbooks now look and behave like the new canonical boundary template rather than like older support-cockpit holdovers.
ADF can review the support-engineer experience across `keycloak-auth`, `messaging-and-data`, `microservice-platform`, and `core-aff` without template inconsistency obscuring the content judgment.
The next Starlight review pass can judge support-language quality and systems-thinking depth on one shared page shell instead of across mixed publication patterns.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the migration would require weakening the page-record contract or hand-authoring boundary pages outside the generator-backed route model.
Stop and escalate if the canonical shell still cannot express a required boundary-specific supplement without reintroducing ad hoc renderer branches.
tasks.summary: Migrate `messaging-and-data`, `microservice-platform`, and `core-aff` from the older support cockpit template path onto the same canonical boundary-playbook shell already used by `ASMS Keycloak auth is down`.

acceptance_criteria: ADF records one explicit cleanup task for the remaining legacy support-cockpit publication path so the old shell is not treated as a quiet second canonical template.
The renderer makes it obvious which shared shell is canonical for boundary-confirmation pages and whether any residual support-cockpit helper survives only as an implementation detail.
Review state tells the next agent that mixed template use across first-class playbooks is an inconsistency to remove, not just an aesthetic difference.
completion_signals: There is one named planner line for retiring or demoting the legacy support-cockpit shell once the remaining boundary pages are migrated.
The next content pass can close the inconsistency instead of re-accepting it as normal renderer variety.
ADF keeps one stable operator-facing playbook shell for comparable boundary pages, which makes support review cleaner and reduces future drift.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if removing the old support-cockpit path would break a still-needed review surface that the canonical boundary shell cannot yet represent.
Stop and escalate if this cleanup starts to widen into broad Starlight redesign rather than a bounded shell-consistency fix.
tasks.summary: Retire or demote the remaining legacy support-cockpit boundary publication path so ADF does not keep two competing first-class playbook shells.

acceptance_criteria: The ADF publication and remote-staging flow stops committing oversized generated binaries, package-manager caches, or preview-build dependencies into normal PackFactory snapshots by default.
The next ADF state snapshot can preserve the operator-facing review surface without carrying heavyweight generated artifacts like preview-site `node_modules` binaries inside tracked staging payloads.
completion_signals: The large-file warning from the full state snapshot has a documented mitigation path in pack-local state.
Staging, preview, or generated-site hygiene rules are tightened enough that future ADF snapshot commits avoid another oversized generated binary warning by default.
The next remote review cycle can still publish the approved Starlight review surface without requiring the tracked heavyweight generated dependency payload that triggered the GitHub warning.
escalation_conditions: The cleanup path would require history rewriting, destructive Git surgery, or broader factory-wide repo policy changes without an explicit operator decision.
The remote review surface would become less reliable or harder to reproduce than the current fail-closed publishing path.
tasks.summary: Tighten ADF staging and generated-artifact hygiene so future state snapshots avoid oversized tracked preview binaries while preserving the approved review surface.

acceptance_criteria: ADF records one explicit plan for transitioning project-goal and objective execution from the local PackFactory orchestrator to the ADF agent running on `adf-dev`.
The plan makes the node-role split concrete: Local Pack Factory stays the canonical planner and evidence accepter, while `adf-dev` becomes the default objective-execution agent for bounded ADF work.
The plan names the minimum control surfaces that must stay local versus the minimum task, memory, and evidence surfaces that may be executed remotely on `adf-dev`.
completion_signals: The planner has one stable task for the orchestrator-to-ADF-agent transition instead of treating it as implied future direction.
Restart state tells the next agent that remote execution ownership on `adf-dev` is now a named planning line, not just a conversational idea.
Later autonomy work can judge progress against an explicit handoff task instead of rediscovering the same transition question.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the transition plan starts to imply silent remote-canonical mode changes without an explicit acceptance rule.
Stop and escalate if the transition plan widens into broad factory-level governance changes instead of a bounded ADF ownership handoff.
tasks.summary: Plan the transition of ADF project-goal and objective execution from the local PackFactory orchestrator to the ADF agent running on `adf-dev`, while keeping PackFactory as the canonical planner and evidence accepter.

acceptance_criteria: ADF has one explicit future-mode specification for making `adf-dev` the canonical home for the ADF build pack without collapsing PackFactory factory governance into the remote host.
The spec names the ownership, state, and tooling changes that would be required before this mode could be treated as active.
The spec says plainly that the remote-canonical ADF mode is not active yet.
completion_signals: ADF records the remote-canonical ADF-home idea as a reviewed future-mode specification instead of leaving it only in conversation.
The planner has a stable task for revisiting this transition later.
Restart state points future agents at the transition note without treating the mode as active.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the planning note starts to silently activate remote-canonical mode instead of documenting it as a future transition.
Stop and escalate if the spec widens into broad factory-governance rewrites instead of staying focused on the ADF pack.
tasks.summary: Define the future-mode transition for making `adf-dev` the canonical home for the ADF build pack while Local PackFactory remains the factory control plane.

acceptance_criteria: ADF defines a stable frontline operator-language contract that favors short literal English, direct branching language, and explicit next-step wording for multilingual support engineers.
The `ASMS UI is down` operator page uses direct check names and avoids documentation-style phrases such as `stop at the UI edge`, `branch out`, or `useful work` in the main frontline flow.
completion_signals: The language contract is recorded in pack-local specs and can guide later playbook edits without rediscovering the same wording issues.
The generated operator page now reads like an explicit triage workflow rather than a design manual or training note.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the language review starts to require a broader product or UX rewrite instead of a bounded wording contract and page update.
tasks.summary: Define and apply a frontline operator-language contract so ADF playbooks read as explicit triage workflows for multilingual support engineers, not as design notes.

acceptance_criteria: ADF defines symptom-entry playbooks, boundary-confirmation pages, and deep guides as separate operator-facing page types.
The page-type contract says plainly that vague customer cases should start from symptom-entry playbooks, not from subsystem pages.
The contract records how Keycloak-like module pages fit as second-stage boundary checks rather than default case-entry pages.
completion_signals: A named pack-local specification records the page-type split and the entry rules for each page type.
The current active content task links directly to the new page-type specification instead of relying on chat-only guidance.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the page-type split starts implying a broad new documentation platform instead of a bounded ADF content contract.
tasks.summary: Define the ADF frontline page-type contract so symptom-entry playbooks, boundary-confirmation pages, and deep guides stay separate.

acceptance_criteria: ADF records an explicit translation rule from internal systems-thinking labels to frontline support language.
The contract says internal architecture labels should not appear in the main diagnostic flow unless support already uses them in daily work.
The contract gives concrete examples of operator-safe wording for symptom checks, service checks, and escalation wording.
completion_signals: A named pack-local specification records the support-language translation rules for future generator and playbook work.
The current active content task links directly to the translation contract instead of rediscovering the same wording issue during review.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the language contract starts requiring a broad UX rewrite instead of a bounded translation and wording rule set.
tasks.summary: Define the support-language translation contract so ADF keeps systems thinking in the model without leaking internal labels into frontline playbooks.

acceptance_criteria: ADF records one explicit systems-thinking workflow for walking the target lab from vague customer symptoms to the first useful support boundary.
The workflow says how to decide whether a finding belongs in a symptom-entry playbook, a boundary-confirmation page, or a deep guide.
The workflow stays evidence-first and bounded instead of widening into open-ended subsystem reverse engineering.
completion_signals: A named pack-local specification records the symptom-to-boundary mapping workflow and the small evidence table it should produce.
The current active content task links directly to the mapping workflow instead of relying on ad hoc phrasing about systems thinking.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the mapping workflow starts requiring deep product reverse engineering before it can guide a first-line support check.
tasks.summary: Define the systems-thinking symptom-to-boundary mapping workflow so ADF can populate pages from real lab journeys without losing frontline support focus.

acceptance_criteria: ADF records a support-safe version of the scientific method for diagnostic playbook work.
The contract explains how observe, hypothesis, test, observe, revise, and repeat should map onto frontline support diagnosis without widening into open-ended reverse engineering.
The contract makes the stop rule explicit so the method remains bounded and support-useful.
completion_signals: A named pack-local specification records the support-safe scientific-method loop for ADF.
The active content task links directly to that note instead of relying on chat-only explanation.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the scientific-method note starts implying academic experiment scope instead of bounded support diagnostics.
tasks.summary: Define the support-safe scientific-method contract so ADF uses observe, hypothesis, test, and stop-point loops without drifting beyond frontline support needs.

acceptance_criteria: ADF records an explicit backend map-and-render contract for how Codex should turn runtime evidence into internal mapping rows, operator-facing rows, page records, and rendered pages.
The contract says plainly that support pages should not be built directly from free-form reasoning.
The contract names the current schema gap around explicit page type, handoff targets, and operator-facing rows.
completion_signals: A named pack-local specification records the backend map-and-render contract for future generator work.
The active content task links directly to that contract instead of treating backend mapping as informal implementation detail.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the backend map note starts requiring a broad schema or product-platform rewrite instead of a bounded ADF generator contract.
tasks.summary: Define the backend map-and-render contract so Codex builds a structured ADF map before the generator turns it into support-facing Starlight content.

acceptance_criteria: The three frontline refinement specs receive an adversarial review for page-type leakage, support-language leakage, and systems-thinking drift.
Any contradictions between the new refinement specs and older ADF guidance are tightened instead of being left for later cleanup.
The review outcome is recorded in a durable pack-local note that future agents can reuse.
completion_signals: The frontline page-type contract, support-language translation contract, and symptom-to-boundary mapping workflow are stricter after adversarial review.
Older linked guidance that still carried analytical operator wording is aligned enough that the pack no longer points in two directions.
A durable review note exists under `eval/history/`.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the adversarial review reveals that the current ADF content model needs a broader product rewrite instead of bounded contract tightening.
tasks.summary: Adversarially review the new frontline refinement contracts and tighten them before more Starlight or generator content changes.

acceptance_criteria: The support-safe scientific-method contract and the backend map-and-render contract receive an adversarial review for ambiguity, overreach, migration risk, and renderer-guessing risk.
Any accepted findings are folded back into the two specs before the next generator or content implementation pass.
The review outcome is recorded in durable pack-local history.
completion_signals: The scientific-method contract is clearer about author-side use, stop rules, and recorded outputs.
The backend map-and-render contract is clearer about ownership, routing, compatibility, and fail-closed rendering.
A durable review note exists under `eval/history/`.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the adversarial review reveals that these two notes require a broader schema or product rewrite instead of bounded contract tightening.
tasks.summary: Adversarially review the support-safe scientific-method and backend map-and-render contracts before the next generator or content implementation pass.
